title: Volumes mounted for container images which run as a specific non-root user should be writeable
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

When running an image that has a specific non-root user built into the image, any volumes mounted using -v are not writeable.  They should be, as they are under Docker.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. podman run an image that has a specific non-root user running the application, such as jupyter/scipy-notebook

2. mount a directory in your homedir using -v, example:

`podman run -p 8888:8888 -v "$PWD":/home/jovyan/work:z jupyter/scipy-notebook:latest`

3. Attempt to write one or more files using the application in the container in that mount.

**Describe the results you received:**

```
[W 19:43:49.128 NotebookApp] 403 POST /api/contents/work/kubevirt (10.0.2.100): Permission denied: work/kubevirt/Untitled.ipynb
[W 19:43:49.129 NotebookApp] Permission denied: work/kubevirt/Untitled.ipynb
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

The file should have been written.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

This happens with all images whose application is a specific non-root user.  Somehow, Docker is able to make it not a problem.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.0.0-dev
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

Not relevant, this is Fedora Silverbue.

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

Tried --security-opt label=disable, had no effect.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: Per Matt Heon, the fix for this is to use `--userns=keep_id`.  Need to get out a blog post showing this common workaround.
Just a small correction for people who end up on this thread, the arg is "keep-id", not "keep_id".
